acousto-electric:
The acousto-electric (AE) effect is the local modulation of electrical conductivity caused by an acoustic pressure wave propagating through a conducting medium such as saline or biological tissue. When ultrasound passes through the material, it produces tiny periodic changes in density, viscosity and ionic mobility that alter conductivity. If an electric current is flowing through the medium at the same time, these conductivity changes create a measurable voltage modulation at the ultrasound frequency, called the acousto-electric interaction (AEI) signal. This hybrid interaction combines the high spatial resolution of focused ultrasound with the high temporal resolution of electrical measurements.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
[3] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
ae effect:
The acousto-electric effect occurs because acoustic pressure changes the physical parameters that control ionic conductivity: adiabatic compression (≈47 %), viscosity change due to pressure (≈18 %), and temperature-induced change in ionic mobility (≈35 %). The overall relative change in conductivity is small (≈0.1 % per MPa in physiologic saline) but becomes detectable when a current flows through the medium. The resulting voltage signal is directly proportional to both the applied acoustic pressure and the current. This effect enables high-resolution mapping of current densities in electrolytes and living tissue.
Reference:
[1] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
[2] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[3] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
aei signal:
The acousto-electric interaction (AEI) signal is the voltage change measured across electrodes when ultrasound alters the local conductivity of a current-carrying medium. In the experimental setup, a constant current is passed through saline while a focused 500 kHz transducer generates pressure up to 1 MPa in the focal zone. After subtracting the Debye (ultrasonic vibration potential) effect that appears even without current, the remaining AEI signal is directly proportional to both acoustic pressure and current. For the cell used in this study, the interaction factor was quantified as 5.3 µV mA⁻¹ MPa⁻¹.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
interaction factor:
The acousto-electric interaction factor quantifies how strongly acoustic pressure modulates voltage in a current-carrying medium. Lavandier et al. measured it experimentally in saline solution and found a value of 5.3 µV mA⁻¹ MPa⁻¹ for their four-electrode cell. In general, the interaction constant K_I is on the order of 10^{-9} Pa^{-1} (0.01 %–0.1 % per MPa) in physiologic saline. This linear relationship allows the AE signal amplitude to be used for quantitative imaging of current density.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
[3] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
debye:
The Debye effect, also called the ultrasonic vibration potential (UVP), generates a potential difference in an electrolyte even when no external current is applied. This potential arises directly from the acoustic pressure field and can distort measurements of the true acousto-electric interaction signal. To isolate the AEI signal, the Debye contribution must be subtracted from the total recorded voltage. This subtraction step is essential for accurate quantification of conductivity changes caused by ultrasound.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
principle:
The principle of the acousto-electric effect is that an acoustic pressure variation dp changes the conductivity of the medium from σ₀ to σ₀ + dσ. For a constant current i flowing between two electrodes separated by distance l and area S, this conductivity change produces a voltage modulation dv = –i (dσ/σ₀²) (l/S). Because dσ is proportional to dp, the equation simplifies to dv = k i R₀ dp, where R₀ is the baseline resistance. The signal therefore appears at the same frequency as the ultrasound and its amplitude scales linearly with both pressure and current.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
[3] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
lobster:
Witte et al. demonstrated acoustoelectric imaging of current flow inside the abdominal segment of a fresh lobster nerve cord placed in a three-compartment nerve chamber. A pair of recording electrodes detected the AE signal induced by focused ultrasound pulses (1.4 or 7.5 MHz) while a 300 Hz sinusoidal current was injected through the nerve. The measured signal was linear with injected current density (0.7 µV/mA·cm² at 2 MPa) and with acoustic pressure (23 µV/MPa at 75 mA/cm²). This experiment proved that the AE effect can map biocurrents with high spatial resolution in neural tissue.
Reference:
[1] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
[2] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
rat heart:
Berthon et al. performed Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging (UAI) on isolated beating rat hearts retroperfused in a Langendorff system. Using a 5 MHz linear array and plane-wave emissions, they mapped biological current densities at frame rates up to 1000 Hz. Strong AE signals were detected in the region of the atrio-ventricular node and ventricular walls, with an average contrast-to-noise ratio of 12.4. Signals appeared at the apex and propagated through the myocardium with a delay of approximately 29 ms, matching the physiological activation sequence even when mechanical contraction was suppressed.
Reference:
[1] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging: application to the beating rat heart
[2] Berthon et al. (2017) An integrated and highly sensitive ultrafast acoustoelectric imaging system for biomedical applications
uai:
Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging (UAI) is a plane-wave ultrasound technique (5 MHz) that achieves high frame rates (up to 1000 Hz) while mapping current densities via the acousto-electric effect. The method uses a research ultrasound platform with 256 channels and high common-mode rejection amplifiers to record the AE signal simultaneously with B-mode images and ECG. UAI provides real-time 2D maps of electrical activation in beating hearts with excellent contrast and temporal resolution. It overcomes the frame-rate limitations of earlier focused-beam AE approaches.
Reference:
[1] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging: application to the beating rat heart
[2] Berthon et al. (2017) An integrated and highly sensitive ultrafast acoustoelectric imaging system for biomedical applications
ucsd:
Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging (UCSDI) exploits the acousto-electric effect to generate 3-D maps of current flow inside conducting media. A focused ultrasound beam scans the volume while boundary electrodes record the modulated voltage. The spatial resolution is determined by the ultrasound focal size, allowing sub-millimeter localization of current sources and sinks. This technique has been validated in saline phantoms and neural tissue and is proposed for real-time cardiac activation mapping during ablation procedures.
Reference:
[1] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
[2] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
in vivo:
Alvarez et al. performed the first in-vivo acoustoelectric cardiac imaging in an open-chest swine model. A 0.6 MHz 2D matrix array was acoustically coupled to the epicardium to image the left-ventricular activation wave. Beat-to-beat variability of the AE signal peak was measured as 5.32 ± 0.74 µV at a depth of 3.26 ± 0.54 mm below the epicardium. Cross-sectional images showed propagation along the epicardial–endocardial axis with velocity 0.192 ± 0.061 m/s and signal-to-noise ratio of 24.9 dB.
Reference:
[1] Alvarez et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging: application to the beating rat heart
conductivity:
Acoustic pressure alters the local electrical conductivity of electrolytes and tissues through changes in density, viscosity and ionic mobility. The relative conductivity change is small (≈0.1 % per MPa in NaCl solution) yet sufficient to modulate voltage when current flows through the medium. This effect is the physical basis for all acoustoelectric imaging modalities. Measurements in saline confirm that the AEI signal is directly proportional to both pressure and current after the Debye potential is removed.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
application:
The main applications of the acousto-electric effect are high-resolution mapping of biological current densities in cardiac and neural tissue. It has been used to image current flow in lobster nerve cords, map activation waves in isolated beating rat hearts, and perform the first in-vivo cardiac imaging in swine. Researchers propose its use during arrhythmia ablation procedures to provide real-time, high-spatiotemporal-resolution maps of electrical activation. It can also support non-invasive neuroimaging and localized brain stimulation concepts.
Reference:
[1] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging: application to the beating rat heart
[2] Alvarez et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[3] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
general use:
The general use of the acousto-electric effect is to create hybrid imaging that combines the spatial precision of ultrasound with the temporal precision of electrical recordings. Ultrasound focuses on a small region and modulates local conductivity, producing a detectable voltage signal proportional to local current density. This allows researchers to map current flow in electrolytes, nerve tissue, and beating hearts without relying solely on surface electrodes. It is particularly useful when high spatiotemporal resolution is needed for dynamic electrical phenomena.
Reference:
[1] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
[2] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[3] Berthon et al. (2017) An integrated and highly sensitive ultrafast acoustoelectric imaging system for biomedical applications
significance:
The significance of the acousto-electric effect lies in its ability to overcome the spatial-resolution limits of traditional EEG/ECG and the invasiveness/contact issues of electroanatomic mapping. It provides non-invasive or minimally invasive high-resolution (sub-millimeter) imaging of current densities inside tissue with excellent temporal resolution (up to 1000 Hz). This makes it a powerful tool for studying cardiac arrhythmias, guiding ablation procedures, and mapping neural activity. It represents a promising step toward real-time, high-fidelity electrophysiological imaging in vivo.
Reference:
[1] Alvarez et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging: application to the beating rat heart
[3] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
measurement:
The acousto-electric effect can be measured by passing a focused ultrasound beam through a conducting medium while simultaneously applying a small electric current. Electrodes placed near the ultrasound focus detect the resulting voltage modulation at the ultrasound frequency (the AEI signal). In practice, a high-frequency lock-in amplifier or oscilloscope is used to extract the tiny AE signal from background noise. Typical setups use 1–5 MHz ultrasound transducers and saline or tissue phantoms. This method provides high spatial resolution because the signal originates only from the focal zone of the ultrasound beam.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Jossinet et al. (1999) Impedance Modulation by Pulsed Ultrasound
[3] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
how to measure:
To measure the acousto-electric effect, apply a low-amplitude DC or low-frequency current across the sample while transmitting pulsed or continuous-wave ultrasound. The ultrasound pressure wave locally modulates conductivity, producing a detectable voltage at the ultrasound frequency. Use differential electrodes positioned at the ultrasound focus and a phase-sensitive detector (lock-in amplifier) to isolate the AEI signal. Signal averaging over many pulses greatly improves signal-to-noise ratio. This technique has been successfully demonstrated in saline, lobster nerve, and living rat heart tissue.
Reference:
[1] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging
[3] Russel et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
equipments:
A typical acousto-electric imaging setup requires a focused ultrasound transducer (1–15 MHz), a function generator for ultrasound pulsing, a current source for the injected electric field, and a pair of recording electrodes. The AEI signal is usually extracted with a lock-in amplifier or high-speed digitizer synchronized to the ultrasound pulse. For in-vivo cardiac work, an ultrafast ultrasound scanner (e.g., Verasonics Vantage) combined with ECG gating is commonly used. Data acquisition and beamforming are performed in real time on a high-performance computer.
Reference:
[1] Russel et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging
[3] Vantage Sequence Programming Manual
resolution:
The spatial resolution of acousto-electric imaging is determined by the ultrasound focal spot size, typically 0.5–2 mm laterally and 1–5 mm axially depending on frequency. Temporal resolution can reach sub-millisecond levels because the AE signal follows the ultrasound pulse in real time. This makes AE imaging far superior to conventional electrical mapping for visualizing rapid cardiac activation wavefronts. Recent ultrafast implementations achieve ~200 µm resolution in the focal zone.
Reference:
[1] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging
[2] Russel et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[3] Jean et al. (2017) An integrated and highly sensitive ultrafast acoustoelectric imaging system
tissue vs saline:
In saline solution the acousto-electric interaction is relatively simple and dominated by ionic mobility changes. In biological tissue the effect is more complex due to additional contributions from cell membranes, extracellular matrix, and blood flow. Tissue conductivity is also frequency-dependent, making the AE signal stronger at higher ultrasound frequencies. Despite these complications, the effect remains detectable and has been successfully imaged in lobster nerve cord and beating rat hearts.
Reference:
[1] Lavandier et al. (2000) Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution
[2] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
[3] Russel et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
safety:
The acousto-electric effect uses diagnostic-level ultrasound intensities (typically < 1 W/cm²) that are well within FDA safety limits for diagnostic imaging. The injected current is also very small (usually < 1 mA/cm²). No adverse biological effects have been reported in the published studies using this technique on living tissue. It is considered a safe hybrid imaging modality for cardiac and neural applications.
Reference:
[1] Russel et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[2] Frontiers in Neuroscience AE review paper
brain:
The acousto-electric effect has strong potential for non-invasive brain mapping because ultrasound can penetrate the skull while electrical signals provide high temporal resolution. Preliminary studies have demonstrated AE detection in neural tissue. It could complement EEG and fMRI by offering millimetre-scale spatial resolution of neural currents. Current research is focused on overcoming skull attenuation and improving signal-to-noise ratio.
Reference:
[1] Frontiers in Neuroscience AE review paper
[2] Witte et al. (2007) Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect
comparison:
Compared with optical mapping or multi-electrode arrays, acousto-electric imaging is completely non-contact and works in opaque tissue. It offers better spatial resolution than surface ECG and higher temporal resolution than MRI. The main limitation is the requirement for simultaneous ultrasound and current injection, which is more complex than pure electrical recording. Overall, it is a promising hybrid modality that combines the strengths of ultrasound and electrophysiology.
Reference:
[1] Olafsson et al. (2008) Ultrasound Current Source Density Imaging
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging
future:
Future developments in acousto-electric imaging include 3D volumetric mapping, real-time closed-loop ablation guidance, and integration with ultrafast ultrasound platforms. Miniaturised transducers and improved signal processing algorithms are expected to enable clinical translation. The technique could revolutionise electrophysiological mapping in cardiology and neurology by providing high-resolution, non-invasive imaging of bioelectric activity.
Reference:
[1] Russel et al. (2020) In vivo acoustoelectric imaging for high-resolution visualization of cardiac electric spatiotemporal dynamics
[2] Berthon et al. (2019) Mapping biological current densities with Ultrafast Acoustoelectric Imaging
[3] Frontiers in Neuroscience AE review paper
